coredata.dc:title: Self-powered triboelectric sensor with N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated polyaniline layer for non-invasive glucose monitoring in human sweat
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Yu-Hsin
Chang, Ching-Cheng
Chang, Ling-Yu
Wang, Pang-Chen
Kanokpaka, Pawisa
Yeh, Min-Hsin
coredata.dc:description: A novel non-invasive wearable self-powered triboelectric sensor (TES) for simultaneous physiological monitoring had shed the light on its extraordinary performance, which become a promising choice for detecting glucose concentration from human sweat via non-invasive strategy. For realizing the function of wearable TES, the flexible substrate of conducting polymers commonly served as matrix for not only immobilizing the specific enzyme, but also played as the sensing layer by the coupling effect of triboelectrification/enzymatic reaction. To boost up the reliability and sensitivity of conducting polymer-based TES, in this study, a nanocomposite of N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated polyaniline (NGQDs/PANI) is designed as a triboelectric layer for non-invasive glucose detection. The surface electronegativity of PANI was changed by decorating the NGQDs with electron-rich functional groups for increasing the surface charge, which further facilitating the charge transfer between the PANI and intermedia and then enhancing triboelectric outputs. Upon the enhanced electron transfer and surface charge by NGQDs, NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES offers greater sensitivity (23.52 mM−1) in glucose detection compared to pristine PANI/GOx one (16.44 mM−1). Notably, this study demonstrates the feasibility of wearable TES on human skin and their reliable and sensitive performance with a self-driving system without any external power source.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-powered
Triboelectric sensors
Non-invasive
Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots
Glucose
Enzymatic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
Preparation of PANI and NGQDs/PANI films
Self-powered NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose detection
Optimization of NGQDs/PANI for the TES
NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose detection
Interference test and repeatability of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose monitoring
Application of self-powered NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose monitoring in human sweat

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan
National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 109-2221-E-011-061-MY2 and 111-2221-E-011-011), and industry-academia cooperation project (NTUST-Might Electronic-No. 9967). The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript. None. The Supporting information contains figures regarding the characterization of the materials.

This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council ( NSTC ) in Taiwan ( MOST 109-2221-E-011-061-MY2 and 111-2221-E-011-011 ), and industry-academia cooperation project ( NTUST -Might Electronic-No. 9967 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of NGQDs; (b) the size distribution of NGQDs; (c) XPS survey of NGQDs; (d) PL excitation-emission mapping of NGQDs, inset: light emission of NGQDs.
(a) The structure of NGQDs/PANI; (b) FTIR of PANI and NGQDs/PANI; (c) the N content of different NGQDs.

(a) Schematic illustration for the working principle of Zeta potential measurement for thin films by surface potential analyzer; (b) pristine PANI and NGQDs/PANI nanocomposite with various N doping levels of NGQDs; (c) the response of different NGQDs/PANI under different H2O2 concentrations.

(a) Working principle of NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES for electrical signal generation during different glucose concentration detection; TENG output when TES detecting 0–2 mM glucose: (b) output Voltage, (c) short-circuit current, and (d) Surface charge; (e) power of pristine PANI and NGQD-3/PANI detecting glucose when operating under different resistances 100 Ω to 10 GΩ; (f) response comparison of pristine PANI and NGQD-3/PANI/GOx.

(a) The output voltage of NGQDs/PANI based TES with/without GOx in the glucose concentration range from 0.5 to 2.0 mM; (b) calibration curve of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose detection of (N = 3); (c) interference test of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose monitoring in the presence of various analytes; (d) repeatability test of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for three individual cycle.

(a) Schematic diagrams of the working mechanism for NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose monitoring in human sweat; (b) demonstration of self-powered glucose monitoring alarm by NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES.
Schematic illustration of non-invasive wearable human sweat TES based on NGQDs/PANI.

head.ce:title: Self-powered triboelectric sensor with N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated polyaniline layer for non-invasive glucose monitoring in human sweat

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. H. Chang and C. C. Chang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel non-invasive wearable self-powered triboelectric sensor (TES) for simultaneous physiological monitoring had shed the light on its extraordinary performance, which become a promising choice for detecting glucose concentration from human sweat via non-invasive strategy. For realizing the function of wearable TES, the flexible substrate of conducting polymers commonly served as matrix for not only immobilizing the specific enzyme, but also played as the sensing layer by the coupling effect of triboelectrification/enzymatic reaction. To boost up the reliability and sensitivity of conducting polymer-based TES, in this study, a nanocomposite of N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated polyaniline (NGQDs/PANI) is designed as a triboelectric layer for non-invasive glucose detection. The surface electronegativity of PANI was changed by decorating the NGQDs with electron-rich functional groups for increasing the surface charge, which further facilitating the charge transfer between the PANI and intermedia and then enhancing triboelectric outputs. Upon the enhanced electron transfer and surface charge by NGQDs, NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES offers greater sensitivity (23.52 mM−1) in glucose detection compared to pristine PANI/GOx one (16.44 mM−1). Notably, this study demonstrates the feasibility of wearable TES on human skin and their reliable and sensitive performance with a self-driving system without any external power source.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, the increasing demand for predictive clinical diagnosis and personalized health care has attracted research efforts on developing reliable sensors on a variety of sensing platforms (e.g., wearable bands, tattoos, and textiles) to address the challenges associated with point-of-care test (POCT) and analysis [1,2]. For real-time human health monitoring, many analytes were detected as biomarkers such as metabolites, proteins, and ions [3,4]. Among the analytes, glucose in bodily fluids is an important biomarker for diagnosing diabetes, one of the world’s most common diseases [5]. In the numerous human fluids, including tissue fluid, sweat, saliva, and tears, sweat was rich in analytes and has the advantage of continuous sampling at multiple places, making it a realistic and appropriate extract for non-invasive monitoring [6,7]. In addition, non-invasive sweat-based glucose sensors emerged as a painless option for continuous and long-term monitoring, replacing traditional invasive sensors [8–10].

In spite of the fact that non-invasive biosensors have been successful in determining whether or not glucose is present in perspiration, the demands placed on power sources in terms of portability, wearability, and integrability have led to an increase in interest in self-powered sensing platforms [11–14]. In order to generate appropriate amounts of electrical power, these endeavors to provide a variety of energy harvesters that can capture solar, thermal, or mechanical energy through human motion [15–18]. However, the energy harvested by solar or thermal would be limited by the condition of the environment, as all the solar cells can only generate electricity during the daytime with sunlight. Compared to the above, triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) are energy harvesting devices that can be used for harvesting various types of mechanical energy in any location and at any time [19–22]. Also, TENGs offer advantages including a wide variety of alternatives, simple to fabricate, low cost, high stability, and can operate in multiple modes [23,24]. Based on the working principle of TENG, which includes the conjugation of electrostatic induction and contact-electrification, the triboelectric layer can be designed via surface modification for realizing the function self-powered sensor [25–27], thereby becoming a self-powered triboelectric sensor (TES).

Among the triboelectric materials for wearable TES, a conductive polymer of polyaniline (PANI) is a preferred candidate for positive triboelectric material due to its low cost, mechanical flexibility, ease of production, and tunable electrical properties [28]. PANI can also modify its reversible electrical and optical properties via redox processes and protonation-deprotonation [29]. Moreover, PANI can be utilized as a substrate for enzyme immobilization in order to provide enzyme stability and make the biosensor more stable [30]. Some studies have reported TESs using PANI as the triboelectric material, Zhao et al. used ultra-stretchable Ecoflex fibers covered with multiwalled carbon nanotubes and PANI as dielectric materials to fabricate TES. Also, three types of enzymes were added to the fibers so that the wearable device could measure the concentrations of lactic acid, glucose, and creatinine in sweat in real-time to find out the metabolic level in vivo [31]. Qin et al. created a cellulose conductive hydrogel-based flexible self-powered perspiration sensor. The hydrogel electrode was made from a cellulose nanocomposite including PANI, TEMPO-oxidized CNFs (TOCNF), and PVA/borax (PVAB). The TOCNF/PANI-PVAB hydrogel was encapsulated in PDMS and then coupled with an ion-selective membrane (ISM) to build a totally flexible self-powered sweat sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity for detecting real-time Na+, K+, and Ca2+ concentrations [32]. Nevertheless, biosensors based on PANI may have poor conductivity in neutral electrolytes, which is the true pH of bodily fluid [33]. To solve the issue, it is necessary to incorporate ternary or quaternary nanocomposite with carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) due to their superior conductivity, high chemical stability, and wide surface area [34]. Additionally, GQDs have unique features such as strong fluorescence activity, low toxicity, robust chemical inertness, improved surface modifications owing to their π-π conjugated surface groups, and superior electrical characteristics [35]. Furthermore, doping heteroatoms such as N, P, B, F, and S will significantly disorient the electron network due to the large difference in electronegativity between carbon and the dopant, resulting in charge distribution and so enhancing the conductivity [36]. Their remarkable charge transport and tunable electronic properties may enhance the conductivity of PANI.

To boost up the reliability and sensitivity of conducting polymer-based TES, in this study, we developed NGQDs/PANI nanocomposite as a triboelectric sensing layer for non-invasively monitoring the glucose level from human sweat. PANI was selected as the triboelectric material due to its ability to be a conductive matrix for enzyme immobilization. To overcome the bottleneck of poor conductivity of PANI in the neutral environment, which was the most common condition for human biofluids, NGQDs was introduced to the matrix of PANI as the triboelectric sensing layer. Furthermore, the doped N atom in the GQDs also helped to increase the surface electronegativity of the PANI, thus improving the sensitivity toward the glucose detection for NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES. Going through the glucose detection of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES, the triboelectric signal was generated via the coupling effect of enzymatic reactions and triboelectrification. By recording the output signal of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES, the concentration of glucose can be evaluated. In addition, the LEDs can be powered up by the NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for serving a visualized indicator of the glucose concentration caution. The proposed NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES could offer alternative medical possibilities for diabetes diagnosis and treatment through continuous non-invasive human sweat analysis. Scheme 1.

All the chemicals utilized in this research are of analytical grade. Aniline (>99%), hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2, 30% w/w in H2O), glutaraldehyde (50%), Glucose oxidase enzyme (GOx, 100,000–250,000 U/g), Sodium phosphate monobasic (NaH2PO4, 99.0%), Sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99.9%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98.0%), and D-(+)-Glucose (C6H12O6, ≥99.5%) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The citric acid (≥99.0%) and uric acid (UA, ≥99.0%) were purchased from Acros Organics. Ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 28–30%) and Sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4, ≥99.9%) were acquired from J.T.Baker. Ascorbic acid (AA, 99.5%) was bought from Pub Chem. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) was obtained from Scharlau. Carbon cloth (W1S1011) was purchased from CeTech.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Using ultrasonication, 40 mg of citric acid was utterly dissolved in 40 mL DI water. To synthesize N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with varied doping levels, varying amounts of ammonium hydroxide (3, 5, 8, and 10 mL) were added to a 40 mL aqueous solution of citric acid (1 mg mL−1). After uniformly disseminating ammonium hydroxide into a well-mixed solution at room temperature (25 ℃), the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated for three hours at 200 ℃. The light yellow solution was formed after cooling to room temperature. The resultant solution was dialyzed with a 3k Da dialysis tube (Spectrum Lab, Inc.) to remove unreacted compounds and impurities; it was designated NGQD-x (x = 1, 2, 3, and 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PANI and NGQDs/PANI films
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of PANI and NGQDs/PANI films [37], carbon cloth was used as the substrate. The plating solution was an aqueous mixture of 1.0 M aniline monomer, 2.0 M HCl, and DI water. NGQDs solutions will replace DI water throughout the NGQDs/PANI plating solutions. A three-electrode setup was set up with a 2 × 2 cm2 of carbon cloth working electrode, a 3.0 M Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a Pt foil counter electrode. Then, as-obtained nanocomposite films were deposited by applying 0.1 mA at charge densities of 30 mC cm−2 to the carbon fabric. After electropolymerization, the material is rinsed with 0.1 M HCl to eliminate any unbound aniline monomer. To immobilize the enzyme on the electrode, 10 μL of GOx solution was added to the electrode by drop-casting method and dry it up in a refrigerator at 4°C.

Morphological images were acquired using Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-6500 F, JEOL) to evaluate the surface morphology of the as-prepared materials. The TEM operation was carried out with the assistance of a cold field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 2100 F, JEOL) that had an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Utilizing Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, PerkinElmer, USA) in the 500–4000 cm−1 wavelength range, the functional groups of the materials were determined. Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (JPS-9030, JEOL), the chemical state of the as-prepared materials was investigated. The spot size was 400 nm, and the X-ray source's energy was 12 kV, 72 W, monochromate Al K at 1486.6 eV. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed on an aqueous dispersion of NGQDs at room temperature. The excitation and emission spectra were recorded using a commercial spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Nanolog-3 spectrofluorometer) with a 20 nm bandpass for both emission and excitation and an InGaAs NIR detector. The PL spectra were scaled based on the measured excitation power. The surface potential of materials was observed by the surface potential analyzer for solid samples (Anton Paar SurPASS 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-powered NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: The self-powered NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES was built up by two triboelectric layers with the Cu based current collector. A friction layer is a PVDF film (thickness = 44 µm) that is double-sided polyimide taped to the Cu top electrode. NGQDs/PANI-TES are contacted by linear motor friction impact at a low frequency of 2 Hz, and the relative humidity is controlled (about ∼45%) to generate a highly reliable electrical signal. In addition, NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES demonstrated its viability by detecting glucose using simple serial LED lights.

NGQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with precursors of citric acid and ammonia hydroxide. The morphology of the as-synthesized NGQDs was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1a, which exhibited a typical spherical shape with a uniform size distribution. Fig. 1b shows the particle size distribution of NGQDs, which revealed the diameters of the NGQDs were primarily dispersed in the 2.5–4.5 nm range with an average diameter of 3.5 ± 0.38 nm. The XPS survey scan of as-synthesized NGQDs (Fig. 1c) confirms the N atoms was doped successfully by showing the presence of the N 1 s (401 eV) peak. Furthermore, the most distinguishing aspect of NGQDs is their unique character of photoluminescence (PL), which could be characterized by recording excitation-emission-intensity spectra, as shown in Fig. 1d. With an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the NGQDs exhibit significant emissions around 440 nm. Following the functionalization of the GQDs with N atoms, there was a red-shift in the excitation wavelength, indicating an electron transfer between the unpaired electrons of the N based functional groups [38]. The lone pair electrons of N functional groups donate electrons to the benzene ring of GQDs, which caused charge redistribution [39]. Moreover, NGQDs aqueous solution displays light-yellow under visible light and emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, as an inset of Fig. 1d shows.

To enhance the electrical conductivity of PANI, NGQDs were introduced into the matrix of PANI. The schematic illustration of NGQDs/PANI nanocomposites and possible mechanism of internal interaction were presented in Fig. 2a. After the electropolymerization of NGQDs/PANI, the NGQDs were uniformly distributed in the matrix of PANI and bonded to the polymer chain by electrostatic force due to the oxygen functional groups in NGQDs [40]. The morphology of pristine PANI and NGQDs/PANI nanocomposites were examined by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. S1.

The pristine PANI exhibited uneven particle morphologies and significant aggregation, but the NGQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer exhibited a porous nanorod-like structure. To study the functionalization effect of the NGQDs with PANI, the pristine PANI and NGQDs/PANI nanocomposite were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2b. FTIR spectra of PANI and NGQDs/PANI nanocomposite are quite similar, which showed characteristic peaks of CC bond vibration at 1597 cm−1 [41]. The sharp peak at 1558 cm−1 is characteristic of the quinonoid ring vibration (N=Q=N) and the 1483 cm−1 peak is related to the benzenoid ring vibration (N-B-N) [42]. The stretching vibration of the C-N bond was interpreted as the peak at 1290 cm−1 [43]. The C–H of the 1,4-disubstituted aromatic ring at 1025 cm−1 [44]. The only difference was that NGQDs had an extra peak at 1105 cm−1 for the C-O-C bond, confirming the presence of NGQDs in the PANI matrix [45]. For further understanding of the function of NGQDs in the matrix of PANI, additional analysis of Raman spectroscopy was conducted, as shown in Fig. S2. The results showed that both of pristine PANI and NGQDs/PANI nanocomposite had a peak at 1161 cm−1, which was attributed to in-plane C-H bending in the PANI quinoid. At Raman shift of 1213 cm−1, a small C-N stretching mode is also found. CN- and C-N- stretching in the quinoid and benzenoid rings led to the Raman shifts at 1460 and 1338 cm−1, respectively [40]. Surprisingly, the electrostatic interaction between PANI chains and NGQDs induced significant Raman shifts from 1368 to 1375 cm−1 and from 1581 to 1585 cm−1, corresponding to NGQDs/PANI C-C and CC stretching [46,47].

For understanding the N doping level effect of NGQDs for PANI matrix, NGQDs with various N doping ratio was synthesized. To control the doping levels of N atom in NGQDs, the volume of NH4OH was modulated as 3, 5, 8, and 10 mL in the synthesized process. According to the volume of NH4OH, the as-synthesized NGQDs were denoted as NGQD-1, NGQD-2, NGQD-3, and NGQD-4. As shown in Fig. S3, high-resolution XPS of N 1 s spectra for as-synthesized NGQDs were deconvoluted into three peaks, which correspond to pyridinic-N (399.4 eV), pyrrolic-N (400.2 eV), and graphitic-N (401.6 eV). As the volume of NH4OH was increased from 3 to 8 mL, the atomic ratio of N for NGQDs increased from 4.77 to 6.21 at%, influencing the N configurations of NGQDs. However, for the case of NGQD-4, the atomic ratio of N (3.03 at%) was inversely related to the volume of NH4OH (10 mL), implying that highly concentrated NH4OH may impede N doping by blocking N bonding with the graphitic N sites [48]. The N dopant has a tendency to incorporate a pyrrolic structure into the N functional groups and is coupled to two carbon atoms [48]. The pyrrolic concentration rose as the weight ratio of the N source increased, as shown in Fig. 2c. Furthermore, the fraction of pyridinic N in the carbocycle increased dramatically, and the structure would be more stable because the bond lengths were shorter [49]. Excess NH4OH, on the other hand, inhibits N doping into aromatic heterocycles, resulting in poor graphitic N content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of NGQDs/PANI for the TES
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to understand the performance of NGQDs/PANI based TES with various N doping levels of NGQDs for glucose detection, the surface electronegativity of pristine PANI and NGQDs/PANI nanocomposites were analyzed by surface potential analyzer. For analyzing the surface potential of NGQDs/PANI nanocomposite, Zeta potential is a measurement to estimate their surface electronegativity. Fig. 3a showed the schematic illustration for the working principle of Zeta potential measurement for thin films by surface potential analyzer. The surface of a charged particle attracts and firmly binds to a thin layer of opposite charge, forming a liquid layer known as the Stern layer; when the particle diffuses in solution, it is involved by an outer diffuse layer composed of loosely associated ions, resulting in the formation of an electrical double layer. The Zeta potential is the potential difference between the main fluid in which a particle is dispersed and the layer of fluid associated with the surface that contains oppositely charged ions [50,51]. Moreover, Zeta potential can be calculated via theoretical models, experimentally determined electrophoretic mobility as described by Eq. 1.
(1)
ζ =
dU
str
d
Δ
Ρ
×
η
ε r
ε 0
×
κ
B
where ζ is the zeta potential, U
s
tr is the streaming potential,
Δ P
is the pressure difference, η is the viscosity,
ε
r
×
ε
0
is dielectric permittivity, the
κ
B
is the electrolyte conductivity [52]. Fig. 3b showed the Zeta potential of pristine PANI and NGQDs/PANI nanocomposite with various N doping levels of NGQDs, which clearly explored the surface electronegativity of NGQDs/PANI increased with increasing the N doping levels in NGQDs. Especially, the NGQD-3/PANI had the highest Zeta potential of − 8.52 mV which may be contributed by abundant pyridinic N from NGQD-3. Pyridinic N in the structure of carbon has been shown to change the electron-withdrawing behavior of the carbon basal plane [53]. The pyridinic N in the NGQDs can decrease the inner electrical resistance of an NGQDs/PANI, allowing additional free electrons and protons to transport within the matrix of PANI [54]. Furthermore, the presence of pyridinic N in the carbon matrix was critical in giving pseudo capacitance properties by imparting negative charges to the carbon surface, enhancing the conductivity of PANI in a neutral environment [55]. After revealing the effect of NGQDs with various N-doping levels for NGQDs/PANI nanocomposites, optimized NGQD-3/PANI was introduced as an triboelectric layer of TES for glucose detection via the GOx enzymatic process, which oxidizes glucose to produce gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [56]. The reaction of PANI and H2O2 has been reported to influence the chemical state of the surface of PANI [57], as shown in Fig. S4. When PANI detects the different concentrations of H2O2, the unstable H2O2 will decompose H+ to induce the protonation of PANI and change the conductivity of PANI [58,59]. Therefore, the conductivity change of PANI could be an critical index to evaluate concentration of H2O2 which derived from glucose oxidation by GOx. The voltage response of NGQDs/PANI based TES with various N doping levels of NGQDs for H2O2 detection was shown in Fig. 3 c. It can be seen that as the concentration of H2O2 increased, the output voltage of NGQDs/PANI based TES also increased. Fig. S5 shows original signal of output voltage of corresponding NGQDs/PANI based TES for H2O2 detection within the concentration range from 0 to 2 mM. Among them, NGQD-3/PANI based TES has the best sensitivity for H2O2 detection, which was consistent with the result of Zeta potential. After understanding the optimized NGQDs/PANI based TES for H2O2 detection, GOx will be introduced to functionalize the triboelectric layer for monitoring glucose levels via triboelectrification/enzymatic reaction coupling effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: Triboelectric sensors could undertake non-invasive self-powered continuous glucose monitoring via specific structural and property changes with the help of the TENG signal transduction concept, which potentially converts kinetic glucose stimuli in human sweat via enzymatic reaction into an electrical signal [31,57]. In this study, contact-separation mode was used for obtaining the output signal of self-powered NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES ( Fig. 4a). The working principle of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES is schematically illustrated in Fig. S6 and described in following. Due to the different electron affinities of the friction materials, when the NGQDs/PANI/GOx and the PVDF with copper come into complete contact for the first time under the action of external force, positive and negative charges accumulate on the surface of the NGQDs/PANI/GOx and the PVDF (Fig. S6 (i)); Once the two friction layers are separated, electrostatic induction generates a potential difference between them, causing electrons to flow from the bottom electrode to the top electrode via load resistance (Fig. S6 (ii)); Following that, electrostatic equilibrium is reached, and no current flows in the external circuit (Fig. S6 (iii)); when the two friction layers contact again, an opposite potential difference is generated, which causes electrons to flow in the opposite direction (Fig. S6 (iv)). In the external circuit, charges are transferred back and forth via continuous contact and separation, generating electrical signals [60]. To understand the optimal immersion time of NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES in the glucose solution, the output voltage of NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES with 1 mM glucose solution for different immersion times was recorded, as shown in Fig. S7. The results showed that the output voltage of NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES will become stable after 60 mins of immersion. Based on the working principle of the TENG, the results of glucose detection within the concentration of 0–2 mM by NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES showed the output signals of voltage (Fig. 4b) and current (Fig. 4c) from 28 to 40 V and 2.5–3.7 μA, respectively. Similarly, Fig. 4d displays the surface charge of NGQD-3/PANI-TES rises when rising the glucose concentration. Fig. 4e demonstrates that NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES exhibited higher instantaneous maximum output of 84 μW at a resistance of 20 MΩ than pristine PANI based TES, which only produces 64 μW at a resistance of 60 MΩ. Moreover, comparing the difference between TES with pristine PANI and NGQDs/PANI/GOx nanocomposite in voltage response under various glucose concentrations (Fig. 4f), it demonstrated that NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES had a higher sensitivity (23.52 mM−1) than pristine PANI one (16.44 mM−1) for glucose detection. Above results clearly confirmed the sensitivity of PANI based TES could be boosted by introducing the NGQDs into the matrix of triboelectric layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference test and repeatability of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the coupling effect of triboelectrification/enzymatic reaction was specifically triggered by the enzyme of GOx for glucose sensing, a comparison of the output voltage of NGQDs/PANI based TES with/without GOx in the glucose concentration range from 0.5 to 2.0 mM, as shown in Fig. 5a. The result revealed the output voltage of the NGQDs/PANI based TES with GOx increased as the concentration of glucose increased, indicated that the enzymatic reaction is crucial in the sensing mechanism of TES. The calibration curve of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES exhibited an increment of voltage output when the glucose concentration deviated, as presented in Fig. 5b. This calibration curve was used to determine the sensitivity of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose detection, which was found to be 23.22 ± 0.41 mM−1 for glucose detection with excellent correlation (N = 3, R2 = 0.98). Furthermore, it is quite crucial for a biosensor to possess a specificity of detection in the human sweat with various kinds of interferents, such as ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), sodium chloride (NaCl), and lactate, which may cause an incorrect measurement. To assess the specificity of glucose detection in human sweat, NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES was immersed in solutions containing AA (1 μM), UA (5 μM), NaCl (0.1 M), lactate (0.011 M), and glucose (500 μΜ and 1 mM) [61]. As demonstrated in Fig. 5c, the signals generated by these interfering analytes were ignorable for NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES. Furthermore, when 1 mM glucose added for detection, the signal of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES exhibited a significant response, indicating that it was highly selective for glucose detection. Meanwhile, the repeatability of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES is also important for practical application of wearable biosensors. Fig. 5d demonstrated that NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES can repeatedly detect glucose concentration with similar output voltage response for detecting the glucose concentration of 2 mM, indicating the outstanding stability and reliability of NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES. After several tests, the GOx activity was not diminished, and it was still possible to determine the concentration with high precision. Traditional disposable glucose sensors have been rendered obsolete by the newly designed NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES, which is capable of realizing self-powered non-invasive glucose sensing from human sweat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of self-powered NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES for glucose monitoring in human sweat
ce:section.ce:para: Human sweat is regarded as one of the most important bio-liquids for non-invasive, continuous monitoring applications. Sweat is one of the available sources with the most sampling points outside the body, ease of collection device placement, and availability of electrolytes and metabolites with physiological significance [8]. To achieve monitoring of the glucose concentration from human sweat NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES, which could be attached on human skin to monitor glucose level for controlling the potential risk for diabetes, as shown in Fig. 6a. Utilizing interfacial of NGQDs/PANI/GOx triboelectric layer and human skin as the two interface layers, the friction between the two layers is used to detect glucose levels. To maintain adequate management of glucose levels (approximately 0–2 mM) to prevent diabetes complications, glucose alert analysis is required to detect dangerously high glucose levels (i.e., hyperglycemia). Therefore, NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES was connected to LED circuit to realize the self-powered glucose monitoring alarm, as shown in Fig. 6b. When the NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES detects a voltage signal with an excessively high glucose concentration (> 2 mM), the LED will light up. This demonstrates that NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES detecting 0 mM glucose can barely light 10 LEDs, whereas detecting 2 mM glucose will light the LEDs, as demonstrated in Video S1. This study demonstrates the feasibility of wearable NGQDs/PANI/GOx based TES on human skin and their reliable and sensitive performance with a self-powered physiological monitering system without any external power source.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2023.108505.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.

Self-powered triboelectric sensor with polyaniline/N-doped graphene quantum dots layer (NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES) has been successfully constructed for non-invasive glucose monitoring in human sweat using a wearable device. Based on the coupling effect of enzymatic reaction and triboelectricity without any external power, the device is able to obtain mechanical energy from human body movement and output frictional electricity detecting signals directly. Due to its flexibility, PANI is commonly applied in wearable biosensors, whereas NGQDs are introduced as electron transfer agents due to their abundance of N-rich functional groups. The GOx in NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES for the enzymatic reaction will produce H2O2, the unstable H2O2 will decompose H+, inducing the protonation of PANI and changing its conductivity and output value. Our results demonstrate that the NGQDs/PANI/GOx nanocomposite successfully increases the surface electronegativity of the triboelectric layer, thereby enhancing the sensitivity of NGQDs/PANI for H2O2 detection. More importantly, for the case with high concentration glucose, which indicated more H2O2 production by GOx, causing the increase of surface charge could induce higher output voltage. To increase the reliability of self-sweat glucose monitoring, NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES display great selectivity for glucose detection, as well as stability, repeatability, and reusability. NGQDs/PANI/GOx-TES provides a medical platform that combines a simple glucose monitoring analogy with an efficient generator mechanism and generates high glucose level alarms for diabetes diagnosis by directly powering LED lights without complex circuits.
The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

Yu-Hsin Chang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ching-Cheng Chang: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Ling-Yu Chang: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Pang-Chen Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Pawisa Kanokpaka: Methodology, Investigation. Min-Hsin Yeh: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 109-2221-E-011-061-MY2 and 111-2221-E-011-011), and industry-academia cooperation project (NTUST-Might Electronic-No. 9967).

ce:section.ce:para: The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
None.
The Supporting information contains figures regarding the characterization of the materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2023.108505.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A wearable electrochemical glucose sensor based on simple and low-cost fabrication supported micro-patterned reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite electrode on flexible substrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xuan, H.S. Yoon, J.Y. Park, A wearable electrochemical glucose sensor based on simple and low-cost fabrication supported micro-patterned reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite electrode on flexible substrate, Biosensors and Bioelectronics 109 (2018), 75–82.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ms. Yu-Hsin Chang is currently a master student in the Department of Chemical Engineering at the National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech), Taiwan, under the supervision of Prof. Min-Hsin Yeh. Her research interests mainly focus on the development of self-powered sensor utilizing triboelectric nanogenerator for physiological detection in the human body.

Mr. Ching-Cheng Chang is currently a master student in the Department of Chemical Engineering at the National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech), Taiwan, under the supervision of Prof Min-Hsin Yeh. His research interests mainly focus on solar-driven photoelectrochromic devices.

Dr. Ling-Yu Chang received her Ph.D. degree in the Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering at the National Taiwan University (NTU) under the supervision of Prof. Jiang-Jen Lin in 2013. After that, she performed as a postdoctoral fellowship in the Prof. Jiang-Jen Lin research group from 2013 to 2014. Her research interests mainly focus on functional polymers design for dye-sensitized solar cells. After that, she works in LCY chemical Corp. for market analysis and product marketing from 2014 to 2023. Currently, she serves as a postdoctoral fellowship at the Department of Chemical Engineering in National Taiwan University of Science and Technology with Prof. Min-Hsin Yeh research group. Her research interests are focused on designing novel functional polymers for electrochemical applications.

Mr. Pang-Chen Wang received his MS degree in Department of Chemical Engineering at National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech), Taiwan, under the supervision of Prof. Min-Hsin Yeh in 2022. His research interests mainly focus on the development of graphene-based PVDF composite for wearable triboelectric nanogenerators.

Ms. Pawisa Kanokpaka received her MS degree in Department of Chemical Engineering at National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech), Taiwan, under the supervision of Prof. Min-Hsin Yeh in 2022. Her research interests focus on the development of self-powered sensor utilizing triboelectric nanogenerator for physiological detection in human body.

Dr. Min-Hsin Yeh received his Ph.D. degree in Department of Chemical Engineering from National Taiwan University (NTU) under the supervision of Prof. Kuo-Chuan Ho in 2013. He then performed as a postdoctoral research fellow at the School of Materials Science and Engineering in Georgia Institute of Technology with Prof. Zhong Lin Wang’s research group (2014–2016). Currently, he is an Assistant Professor in Department of Chemical Engineering of National Taiwan University of Science and Technology since 2018. His research interests are focused in the area of the nanomaterials and their applications in electrochemistry, energy harvesting systems, energy storage, and self-powered triboelectric sensors.
